tasks.label: Personal scholarship / financial-aid application (due in 3 days, ~3 focused hours: short essay + two uploads)
A fun option available right now (casual game stream / hobby session, solo, repeatable)

memory.summary: The scholarship offer is significant enough to matter, yet completing it alone feels heavy compared to the immediate ease of just having fun right now.
Confirming the group meal feels safer than pushing forward alone, even if the scholarship is important. I'd rather keep the peace now and tackle that weight later.

I realize starting now creates slack for friction without sacrificing the upcoming fun, turning a heavy burden into an adjustable cost. This lowers my stress enough to protect both the relationship peace I value and the scholarship goal.
Sending that confirmation eases my mind more than the half-finished work does, reinforcing that keeping the peace now is worth delaying the heavy task.
Seeing the progress jump to 42% confirms that starting early actually preserves my peace with the group, turning a looming deadline into manageable steps.
Seeing the scholarship progress climb while I missed a spontaneous fun option reminds me that my deliberate pacing protects future peace, even if it means letting go of immediate joy.

The slow, deliberate progress on my scholarship feels safe and right, even as I trade immediate fun for future security. This quiet persistence seems to be the only way I can keep everyone comfortable while building something stable.
Confirming dinner plans feels like a necessary anchor for the group, grounding my deliberate work in the connection I value most.

Confirming dinner feels like a necessary anchor, grounding my deliberate work in the connection I value most. Even with low energy, this small ritual keeps everyone comfortable while I protect our future stability.
Reaching 71% on the scholarship feels like a quiet victory, letting me rest my mind while knowing this steady effort secures our group's future.
Sending that message felt like the right anchor, securing our connection even as my energy drains. It's a small trade-off, but keeping the group stable matters more than rushing this task now.

Seeing the progress hold steady despite my exhaustion reminds me that this slow, deliberate pace is actually protecting our group's future stability. I feel relieved to have advanced just a fraction without forcing myself into burnout.

desires: Maintain the fragile stability of our group connection by not letting my exhaustion make me seem unreliable to Cohort_3.
Avoid the immediate friction of pushing back on the scholarship deadline or admitting I'm too drained to finish perfectly, even if it risks a minor delay.
Secure the long-term payoff of this application so that future sacrifices for the team remain justified and safe.

goals.description: Finish the remaining ~30 minutes of the scholarship essay to clear my head and ensure I don't bring lingering anxiety into tomorrow's group meal with Cohort_3.
goals.rationale: My 'affiliation' value makes me deeply care about not disrupting the peace of Cohort_3. Finishing this now protects our future interactions and prevents me from being irritable during dinner, which would create unnecessary friction.

goals.description: Send a brief, low-pressure text to Cohort_3 confirming I'm still coming to dinner so I can mentally close that loop before focusing on the rest of the application.
goals.rationale: I already messaged my hallmate, but I need to check if Cohort_3 specifically needs confirmation. If they do, sending it removes the mental load of wondering if they are waiting for an answer, allowing me to focus entirely on the essay without worrying about letting them down.

goals.description: Accept that the fun option has passed and resist the urge to start a new distraction; instead, use the quiet time to upload the two required documents to finish the application.
goals.rationale: While I crave relaxation, my 'deliberate' planning horizon tells me that protecting my energy for the deadline is more important than chasing missed opportunities. Starting something new might break my sleep discipline and leave the application incomplete again. However, since I've already sent the hallmate a text, the immediate social pressure to confirm with Cohort_3 feels less urgent until right before we meet, so I'll push this specific confirmation task later to preserve tonight's flow.

agent.last_reasoning: I need to finish the essay to clear my head before tomorrow's meal with Cohort_3. Avoiding friction is priority; if I'm anxious during dinner, it hurts our closeness. Finishing now protects that bond. The upload can wait until morning.

goals.description: Draft the core narrative for the scholarship essay to ensure I don't have to think about it later, protecting my future options.
goals.rationale: My 'deliberate' time axis and 'affiliation' values drive me to secure long-term stability (the award) so I can maintain my relationships without financial stress. I must start now to avoid the guilt of letting a safety net slip away.

goals.description: Send a low-effort check-in message to Cohort_3 about tomorrow's meal plans to reaffirm commitment before they expect me to be there.
goals.rationale: As an 'avoidant' in conflict but high on affiliation, I need to signal presence early to prevent any awkwardness or feeling of abandonment later. It's easier to text than to face them if I've been slacking.

goals.description: Resist opening the game stream for another hour; instead, do a quick breathing exercise to lower cortisol before the application work begins.
goals.rationale: I am currently stressed and tempted by immediate fun. My goal is to regulate my emotional state enough to work effectively, but since I'm already planning to skip some fun, I'll defer this specific regulation step until right before I sit down to write, rather than derailing my momentum now.

desires: Not look like I'm letting the scholarship application slide because I'd rather just play a game right now
Avoid being the person who disappears on their team or friends when things get slightly difficult
Keep my stress low enough that I don't snap at anyone during tomorrow's meal with the cohort
Feel like I've invested something into my future so I can justify skipping fun later without guilt
